tasks.task_name: Find warm backpacking destination (1-2 night, mid-March)
tasks.hook_line: Can you find a warm trail that won't be snow-covered in 11 days?
tasks.reward: Unlocks flight booking and gear planning — the whole trip structure falls into place
nuggets: Southern Arizona/Texas stay warm in March
Big Bend gets booked early—check now
Red River Gorge KY highs around 60°F
tasks.system_prompt: Help me find a warm backpacking destination for a 1-2 night trip around March 14-15, 2026. I need somewhere with daytime temps above 55°F and minimal snow. Flying from NYC, so accessible airports matter. Looking for established trails with beginner-friendly camping spots (this is for a friend's 30th birthday celebration). Give me 3-5 options with temperature forecasts, trail difficulty, permit requirements, and nearest airports with flight times from NYC.

tasks.first_domino: Open Google Flights to [destination from task 1]
tasks.hook_line: Destination locked means this is pure execution — compare prices and book
tasks.reward: First leg of the trip is real — locked dates and real confirmation numbers
nuggets: Book NYC-destination Mar 14 morning
Return flight Mar 15 evening or 16 morning
Southwest often best for last-minute changes
tasks.system_prompt: I need to book round-trip flights from NYC to [destination] for March 14-15, 2026. Departing NYC morning of March 14 (ideally before 9am), returning evening of March 15 or morning of March 16. I'm flexible on airlines but prefer direct flights and reasonable prices. After this trip I need to fly to Chicago by March 16 evening. Show me the best 3-4 flight options with prices, layovers, and total travel time.

tasks.task_name: Book flight from backpacking to Chicago
tasks.first_domino: Open Google Flights [backpacking city] to Chicago
tasks.hook_line: Final piece of the flight puzzle — then the whole itinerary is locked
tasks.reward: Complete flight chain is booked — no more travel logistics to think about
nuggets: Arrive Chicago by Mar 16 evening
United/American hub Chicago—many options
Already have Chicago-NYC return booked
tasks.system_prompt: I need to book a one-way flight from [backpacking destination] to Chicago arriving by evening of March 16, 2026. This connects a backpacking trip (Mar 14-15) to a birthday celebration in Chicago (Mar 16-19). I already have my return flight Chicago to NYC booked for March 23. Show me the best flight options from [destination] to Chicago ORD or MDW, prioritizing arrival time and reasonable prices.

tasks.task_name: Figure out REI gear rentals (backpack, tent, sleeping bag)
tasks.hook_line: REI rents complete backpacking kits — never done this before, could save hundreds
tasks.reward: Know exactly what gear you need to rent vs buy — no scrambling at the last minute
nuggets: REI rents backpack+tent+bag packages
Reserve online, pick up in-store
Typically $50-100 for weekend rental
tasks.system_prompt: Help me understand REI's backpacking gear rental program. I need a backpack, tent, and sleeping bag for a 1-2 night trip in mid-March 2026 (warm climate, temps 50-70°F). I'm in NYC and there's an REI in SoHo. Walk me through: 1) What gear packages they offer 2) Pricing structure 3) How far in advance to reserve 4) Pickup/return process 5) Whether I need anything else (sleeping pad, stove, etc). This is my first time renting backpacking gear.

tasks.first_domino: Text Mariano's girlfriend for gift ideas
tasks.hook_line: What do you get someone turning 30 who you're already backpacking with?
tasks.reward: Gift is thoughtful and ready — no last-minute airport shopping
nuggets: Backpacking trip itself is a gift
Consider Chicago-specific experience gift
30th birthdays lean toward meaningful over stuff
tasks.system_prompt: Help me brainstorm a birthday gift for my friend Mariano who's turning 30. Context: I'm already organizing a backpacking trip and going to Chicago for his birthday celebration March 16-19. He's outdoorsy, into backpacking/camping, and we're close friends. Budget around $50-100. Should I even get a separate gift beyond the trip? If yes, what would be thoughtful but not over-the-top? Consider: Chicago experiences, outdoor gear he might not buy himself, or something memorable for his 30th.

tasks.first_domino: Open Google Maps and search 'Kyoto temples'
tasks.hook_line: Kyoto has 2000+ temples — which ones are actually worth the early wake-up?
tasks.reward: First city in Japan is fully planned — hotel to hotel with walking routes
nuggets: Fushimi Inari free entry, open 24hrs
Arashiyama bamboo grove best before 9am
Kinkaku-ji ¥500 entry, opens 9am
tasks.system_prompt: Help me plan activities for 2.5 days in Kyoto (March 25-27, 2026). Traveling with my girlfriend Dana. We like temples, walking, outdoor spaces, and good food. Already have hotels booked in central Kyoto (Travelodge Kawaramachi). I want a day-by-day itinerary with opening hours, Google Maps links, and a suggested walking route. Include a mix of major landmarks (Fushimi Inari, Golden Pavilion, Arashiyama) and less touristy spots. Note: We arrive from Osaka airport around noon on March 25.

tasks.task_name: Look up eyeglasses stores in Kyoto for early trip shopping
tasks.hook_line: Can you find English-friendly eyeglass shops near your Kyoto hotel before you land?
tasks.reward: Day 1 or 2 in Kyoto includes glasses shopping — get prescriptions sorted early
nuggets: JINS and Zoff are major chains with English support
Kyoto Porta underground mall has Zoff location
Typical fitting/purchase takes 30-60 minutes
tasks.system_prompt: Help me find eyeglass stores in Kyoto near Kawaramachi (where I'm staying March 25-27). I want to buy 4 pairs of prescription glasses during my Japan trip. Need stores that: 1) Have English-speaking staff or easy processes for foreigners 2) Can do same-day or next-day service 3) Are reasonably priced (JINS/Zoff type chains are fine). Give me 3-5 store locations with addresses, hours, Google Maps links, and what to expect for the buying process. I have a US prescription and insurance that covers international purchases.

tasks.task_name: Validate insurance/prescription requirements for buying 4 pairs of glasses
tasks.first_domino: Call insurance: 'I'm buying glasses in Japan—what's the claim process?'
tasks.hook_line: 20 days out — if insurance needs paperwork, you need to know now
tasks.reward: No surprises at the store — know exactly what receipts/forms you need
nuggets: Most US vision insurance covers international
Need itemized receipts in English
Prescription must be current (within 1-2 years)
tasks.system_prompt: I'm buying 4 pairs of prescription eyeglasses in Japan in late March 2026. Help me figure out: 1) What documentation I need from my US eye insurance to file claims for international purchases 2) Whether my current US prescription is valid for Japan purchases or if I need anything translated/certified 3) What receipts/paperwork to request from Japanese stores to make reimbursement smooth 4) If there are any limits on number of pairs covered per year. My insurance is standard US vision coverage (likely VSP or similar). I want to make sure I can buy 4 pairs and get reimbursed without issues.

tasks.hook_line: Takayama is the Japanese Alps base — what hikes are snow-free in late March?
tasks.reward: Mountain town itinerary locked — know what gear to pack for altitude
nuggets: Old town Takayama preserved Edo-era streets
Shinhotaka Ropeway views of Alps ¥3200
Morning markets 7am-noon daily
tasks.system_prompt: Help me plan 2-3 days in Takayama in late March/early April 2026. I'm traveling with Dana, coming from Kyoto. We like hiking, nature, and traditional towns. Takayama is our Japanese Alps stop. Need: 1) Day-by-day activity ideas (hiking trails safe in early spring, scenic spots, old town walking) 2) Weather expectations and gear needs 3) Day trip options (Shirakawa-go, ropeway, etc) 4) Food recommendations. Note: We're moderately fit but not expert hikers. Give me a mix of outdoorsy and cultural activities.

tasks.hook_line: Tokyo is massive and overwhelming — which neighborhoods match your actual interests?
tasks.reward: Final leg of Japan trip is planned — the whole itinerary is complete
nuggets: Shinjuku for skyscrapers, nightlife, neon
Asakusa for old Tokyo temples, Sensoji
Shibuya crossing photo op, 8am best time
tasks.system_prompt: Help me plan 4 days in Tokyo (April 3-7, 2026) for two first-time visitors. Dana and I like: food, walking neighborhoods, unique shops, some urban energy but not overwhelming crowds. Not super interested in anime/gaming culture. Need: 1) Neighborhood guide (which areas to prioritize) 2) Day-by-day loose itinerary with mix of major sights and chill wandering 3) Restaurant recommendations (mix of price points) 4) Fountain pen stores (I'm shopping for limited edition Sailor pens) 5) What to skip. We've already done temples in Kyoto, so Tokyo should feel different.

tasks.first_domino: Open VS Code task-pilot workspace in Cursor
tasks.hook_line: You started this yesterday — the architecture is fresh in your head
tasks.reward: Extension core is functional — can start testing with real tasks
nuggets: VS Code extension API docs in sidebar
Cursor already configured for this workspace
Core file structure already scaffolded
tasks.system_prompt: I'm building a VS Code extension called AI Task Pilot to help with AI-assisted development while maintaining task focus. Core concept: visualize work in progress and provide AI with context of ongoing work while editing a shared document locally. This is an alternative to Craft agent system which doesn't integrate well with Cursor projects. I started development yesterday (March 2). Continue working on the core features: 1) Task tracking and visualization 2) Shared document management 3) AI context injection. Use VS Code Extension API best practices. Language: TypeScript. Help me prioritize what to build next based on MVP needs.

tasks.first_domino: Sketch task card layout on paper or Figma
tasks.hook_line: Can you make task cards that work in a VS Code sidebar panel?
tasks.reward: Clear UI direction — know exactly what to build instead of coding blind
nuggets: VS Code webview panels support HTML/CSS/JS
Tree view vs custom webview trade-offs
ADHD-friendly design needs low visual noise
tasks.system_prompt: I'm building a VS Code extension for task management targeting ADHD developers (me). I need to design a task visualization UI that works within VS Code's constraints (sidebar panel, limited width). Design goals: 1) Show active tasks at a glance 2) Low visual clutter (ADHD-friendly) 3) Quick actions without leaving editor 4) Visual progress indicators. Help me decide: tree view (native VS Code) vs custom webview panel? Then sketch out the task card design: what info to show, how to indicate status, what actions to expose. Consider that this pairs with AI chat — tasks need to be easy to reference.

tasks.first_domino: Write spec: what context does AI need per task?
tasks.hook_line: How do you package task context so AI stays focused across conversations?
tasks.reward: Core value prop works — AI can maintain context across editing sessions
nuggets: Context = task description + file refs + chat history
Cursor API for injecting context into chat
Shared markdown file as persistent task log
tasks.system_prompt: I'm building a VS Code extension that helps AI stay focused on specific tasks while coding. The core challenge: when I switch between Cursor AI chats, the AI loses context about what I'm working on. I want to build a context management system that: 1) Captures task state (description, relevant files, progress notes) 2) Injects context into new AI conversations 3) Maintains a shared document both human and AI can reference. Design the data structure and API for this context management system. Consider: what context is essential vs noise? How to serialize it? How to inject into Cursor/Claude conversations? TypeScript implementation.

recommended_slots.reason: Most urgent deadline (10 days) blocks entire Chicago trip planning chain
High-impact Japan planning during medium-effort ramp_up energy band
Quick research win (30 min, low context) — palette cleanser from desk work
